item.title: on the virtues of less
item.description:
I was staring at a pull request the other day — forty-seven files changed, a new abstraction layer, a new container where previously there had been none, and all the associated container build and run mechanisms. The commit message said “refactor for extensibility”.

This is not a story about that particular pull request, it is about the instinct that produced it, and about a maxim I have come to regard as among the most useful and least heeded in software engineering.

item.title: Is there prior art for the CDD concept?
item.description:
I used a collaborative development session to find support for my claim of novelty. I find Response 3 interestingly because it asks the Agent to tell me how to better use the Agent.

date: 2026-03-13 title: cdd literature review current_head: f3ffc89f6b9983f31fdd3c141f30b740f0107f83 agent: Copilot Claude Opus 4.6 session_type: instructions: _cdd-instructions.md topics: []

cdd literature review

Prompt 1: find prior art related to CDD

Because I have not seen anything like it, I believe that the CDD concept is novel. Look at the collaborative-development-docs and cdd-revisited and then search the web for similar approaches to working with agentic AI in software development. Create an annotated bibliography of your results

item.title: Bedrock Input Error Handling
item.description:
This is a diagnostic investigation into an error encountered when using Prima-Code with Bedrock models. There is a developer prompt followed buy the Copilot’s analysis.

Prompt 1: explore the logfire traces

There is an Issue #288 Prima-Code Not Working with Bedrock Models that describes a problem when using Prima-Code with Bedrock models.

I have reproduced the fault and there is a logfire trace 019bfc9786f2a881dcb5e827d56f083e that shows the error. Read through the trace and see if you can determine the cause.

item.title: Bedrock Input Error Handling - Take 2
item.description:
This is a continuation of the exploration in 2026-01-26-bedrock-input-error.md. The fix performed did not work. I have some more data to explore.

Prompt 1: summarize 2026-01-26-bedrock-input-error.md for context

So you have information you need without re-reading the previous exploration, please summarize the key points from 2026-01-26-bedrock-input-error.md for context. You are the consumer of this summary so make it suitable for your purposes.

Response 1: summary so far

Issue and Root Cause

Issue #288 reports Prima-Code failures with Bedrock models. Investigation of Logfire trace 019bfc9786f2a881dcb5e827d56f083e identified the root cause:

item.title: making python packages the modern way
item.description:
Although the [uv package and project management tool][uv] has been taking the Python community by storm, I’ve found its documentation to be annoying incomplete. This is the first in a series of notes that I’m writing to address some places of chronic misunderstandings I’ve seen in the community.

The first of these is how to handle imports within the package being written under uv. I’ve not found a place where this is described clearly and adds to the longstanding Python problems of getting relative imports correct.

item.title: a plea for better developer documentation
item.description:
This notion has been in my head for years, but seeing the instructions for Installing Rust on Linix made me want to write this down.

The cut-and-paste instructions are

curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh

Now curl https://some.site/some.sh | sh is mildly spooky on its own, running a script from the internet. But, the Rust team has a good reputation and we can assume that the script is safe. But then why the additional flags on curl? What do they do? Why are they there? Do I need them?

item.title: a clear shortcut
item.description:
When sharing terminal screens in video conferencing, I often see people typing

clear

in the shell to clear the screen. There is a better way.

Since as long as I can remember, possibly dating back to the advent of GNU Bash and its readline line editor, Ctrl-L has been present to clear the screen. To use it, press

Ctrl-L

The historically minded will remember that Ctrl-L is the ␌ ASCII form-feed character that would eject a page when sent to a Teletype.

item.title: About Me
item.description:
matt wartell writes software during the day and sentences at night, both with more confidence than is probably warranted.

He has been programming long enough to have watched entire paradigms arrive with great fanfare, achieve dominance, and settle into legacy status—all without the universe noticeably caring. He finds this comforting, mostly.

His essays, collected here under the name flotsam and…, were written between 2005 and 2012. They concern themselves with the usual subjects: why /bin/true is surprisingly interesting if you follow it far enough back through Unix history; what “kk” actually means in a text message and whether the distinction matters (it does); the mechanics of how good people end up defending bad systems; and occasional dispatches from Minecraft, where he spent considerable time failing to punch trees into boards and emerging from the experience with opinions.

item.title: annotated exemplar: fixing field mismatches
item.description:
---
date: 2026-02-18
title: fixing field mismatches
current_head: 679a0d47a64fc9686a26ba0cc1f91cd20bb45be7
agent: Copilot Claude Sonnet 4.5
session_type:
instructions: _cdd-instructions.md
topics: [pydantic, schema, cip, field-naming, model-generation]
---
frontmatter: anchoring the session current_head records the exact git commit the agent was working from, making the session reproducible. instructions delegates response-formatting rules to a shared file, keeping boilerplate out of the CDD itself. Together these two fields answer: "what was the agent looking at, and what were the rules of engagement?"

Prompt 1: understanding the issue

While creating and testing ogma.ingest.backwards we found that there were surprising mismatches between ogma.ingest.cip_models and schema/cip_schema.yaml
